title: Death rate by cause
subtitle: The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people from broad categories of causes of death. Injuries (such as accidents, violence and suicides); communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases; and [non-communicable diseases](#dod:non-communicable-diseases).
caption: Between 1980 and 2023, the estimated annual global death rate per 100,000 people fell from roughly 1,300 to around 700, with the decline distributed across three broad cause categories stacked in an area chart. The pink base layer, representing non-communicable diseases (NCDs), begins near 800 in 1980 and trends downward to approximately 500 by 2023, forming the largest component throughout the period. Above it, the green band for communicable, maternal, neonatal, and nutritional diseases starts at about 400 and contracts steadily to roughly 200 by the end of the range. The tan top layer, labeled injuries, remains the smallest segment, hovering between 100 and 150 for most of the timeline, with a visible dip around 2020 followed by a rebound toward 2023. All three layers show net reduction over the 43-year span, though the pace of decline varies: the green layer shrinks more rapidly after 2000, while the pink layer’s descent slows after 2010. A noticeable interruption occurs around 2020, where the total height dips before rising again in 2021–2022, then falling once more in 2023. The vertical axis spans from 0 to 1,400, marked in increments of 200, and the horizontal axis marks decades from 1980 to 2023, with finer gridlines every ten years. The data is age-standardized, allowing comparison across time without distortion from population aging. The dominant pattern visible is a sustained, multi-decade reduction in mortality across all cause categories, with non-communicable diseases consistently accounting for the largest share of deaths per 100,000 people worldwide.
